title: Miss Edith Makes It Pleasant for Brother Jack
content:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Crying!ï؟½ Of course I am crying, and I guess you would be crying, too,
آ آ آ آ If people were telling such stories as they tell about me, about you.
آ آ آ آ Oh yes, you can laugh if you want to, and smoke as you didnï؟½t care how,
آ آ آ آ And get your brains softened like uncleï؟½s. Dr. Jones says youï؟½re gettinï؟½ it now.

آ آ آ آ Why donï؟½t you say ï؟½Stop!ï؟½ to Miss Ilsey? She cries twice as much as I do,
آ آ آ آ And sheï؟½s older and cries just from meanness, for a ribbon or anything new.
آ آ آ آ Ma says itï؟½s her ï؟½sensitive nature.ï؟½ Oh my! No, I shaï؟½nï؟½t stop my talk!
آ آ آ آ And I donï؟½t want no apples nor candy, and I donï؟½t want to go take a walk!

آ آ آ آ I know why youï؟½re mad! Yes, I do, now! You think that Miss Ilsey likes you,
آ آ آ آ And Iï؟½ve heard her repeatedly call you the bold-facest boy that she knew;
آ آ آ آ And sheï؟½d ï؟½like to know where you learnt manners.ï؟½ Oh yes! Kick the table, thatï؟½s right!
آ آ آ آ Spill the ink on my dress, and go then round telling Ma that I look like a fright!

آ آ آ آ What stories? Pretend you donï؟½t know that theyï؟½re saying I broke off the match
آ آ آ آ Twixt old Money-grubber and Mary, by saying she called him ï؟½Crosspatch,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ When the only allusion I made him about sister Mary was, she
آ آ آ آ Cared more for his cash than his temper, and you know, Jack, you said that to me.

آ آ آ آ And itï؟½s true! But itï؟½s me, and Iï؟½m scolded, and Pa says if I keep on I might
آ آ آ آ By and by get my name in the papers! Who cares? Why, ï؟½twas only last night
آ آ آ آ I was reading how Pa and the sheriff were selling some lots, and itï؟½s plain
آ آ آ آ If itï؟½s awful to be in the papers, why, Papa would go and complain.

آ آ آ آ You think it ainï؟½t true about Ilsey? Well, I guess I know girls, and I say
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s nothing I see about Ilsey to show she likes you, anyway!
آ آ آ آ I know what it means when a girl who has called her cat after one boy
آ آ آ آ Goes and changes its name to anotherï؟½s. And sheï؟½s done it and I wish you joy!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
